widgets_values:
# Prompt Strategy

The goal of this system is to turn a prompt strategy into an automated system.

If you don't have a prompt strategy you can ask an LLM like ChatGPT for one. I asked windows CoPilot for the following:

```
Give me a prompt strategy for pictures of a elf that can be in different locations wearing different sets of clothes and in different poses
```

## This is what it gave me:

🧝‍♀️ Modular Prompt Strategy: "Elf in Context"
🔹 Base Structure
"A [adjective] elf [action/pose], wearing [clothing description], in [location], [lighting/style cues]"


🔹 Prompt Modules
1. Elf Descriptor
- Gender/Type: female elf, male elf, forest elf, high elf, dark elf
- Adjectives: elegant, mischievous, battle-ready, regal, mysterious, youthful, ancient
2. Pose / Action
- Standing with arms crossed
- Sitting on a tree stump
- Casting a spell
- Walking through mist
- Drawing a bow
- Dancing under moonlight
3. Clothing / Outfit
- Flowing green robes with leaf embroidery
- Silver armor with glowing runes
- Casual tunic and boots
- Winter cloak with fur trim
- Festival attire with floral crown
- Hooded cloak with leather straps
4. Location / Setting
- Enchanted forest
- Snowy mountain pass
- Ancient ruins
- Elven city at dusk
- Moonlit glade
- Misty swamp
- Crystal cave
5. Lighting / Style
- Soft golden hour lighting
- Dramatic shadows and moonlight
- Ethereal glow with sparkles
- Cinematic fantasy realism
- Painterly style with rich textures

🔹 Example Prompts
- "A regal high elf drawing a bow, wearing silver armor with glowing runes, in an ancient forest temple, cinematic lighting"
- "A mischievous forest elf dancing under moonlight, dressed in festival attire with a floral crown, in a moonlit glade, ethereal glow"
- "A battle-ready dark elf standing with arms crossed, wearing a hooded cloak and leather straps, in a misty swamp, dramatic shadows"

# Step 1 - Lay down the strategy

## We can start by laying down the strategy in a ***Mpi Prompt Processor*** node. This is the node where it all ends and gets processed, and also the node that receives your strategy.

### As it stands, the strategy given to us by CoPilot was as follows:
```
A [adjective] elf [action/pose], wearing [clothing description], in [location], [lighting/style cues]
```

## ***Mpi Prompt Processor*** looks for words written in a certain way and replaces them according to it's connected _prompt lists_.

## So we have to change this strategy for it. 👇👇👇

# Step 2 - Create the inputs (Lists)

## So far we can clearly see that we need 5 lists of options, so let's create them using the ***Mpi Prompt List*** node and using the lists CoPilot provied  us.

## For the elf type we where provided with 2 things, ***Adjectives*** and ***Gender/Type*** we will tackle this later but for now we shall do 2 lists for this.

# Step 4 - Processing
## Ok now we can connect our initial ***Mpi Prompt Processor*** to the end of the chain and see the result.
A Walking through mist, wearing Hooded cloak with leather straps, in a Snowy mountain pass. Painterly style with rich textures.

## Ok so we can see that all our \_triggers_ have been replaced correctly but we still need to address the \_elf_type_ trigger.

## !Notice that \_elf_type_ is gone, this is because the ***Mpi Prompt Processor*** _"cleanup"_ option is turned on, with it off, you can spot un-used trigger words

# Step 3 - Connecting

## The ***Mpi Prompt List*** node has handy inputs and outputs that allow us to share seeds and create chains of lists so lets connect them all in to 1 single chain.

## I will leave the first 2 separate as I intend to convert them into 1 single trigger later.

# Step 6 - See the result

## Ok now I really want to see what this will create, so I will connect it all up to a generator and check it out 😍😍
👈 By placing "elf type" in the "opt_title" field we guarantee we can call it later by using _elf_type_ in another prompt processor!

nodes.title: Prompt Processor and types of triggers!
widgets_values:
Let's consider the list you are trying to trigger is named "locations" and has the following options:

- Car Park
- Movie Theater
- Forest

In a prompt processor you can trigger an option on this list in 3 ways:
- ***Single item***: \_locations_

This will trigger a single item from your list like: 
```
I went to the _locations_
Possible Output: I went to the Forest
```

- ***Multiple items***: \_locations_x3_

This will trigger 3 items from your list like: 

```
Various locations like the _locations_x3_
Possible Output: Various locations like the Movie Theater, Car Park, Forest
```

- ***Single pick***: \_locations_2_

This will trigger option 2 from your list, useful for when you want to trigger more than 1 item:

```
I went to the _locations_1_ and to the _locations_2_.
Possible Output: I went to the Forest and to the Car Park.
```
List A option 1 negative, List B option 2 negative.
List A option 2 positive, List B option 2 positive.

## Mpi Prompt Display
### This node can be very useful to find out what is reaching the end of a chain. 
- ***title***: Displays all available lists
- ***blocks***: Displays all lists that meant to be blocked
- ***processed_prompts***: Displays all available processed prompts

# Step 5 - Converting to single output
## There's a few ways to convert lists into a single output and one of them is by using a ***Mpi Prompt Processor*** like below.

## We connect a chain to it and then place a trigger word in the _"opt_title"_ field. This will allow us to use its result with a single trigger word.
I added an "A " prefix here so that all the items in the list are prefixed by "A ". This is a handy way to alter the hole list without having to change all options manually. 👉👉👉👉👉👉👉👉👉👉👉👉👉👉
